metadata.title: Algorithms to Live By (Christian & Griffiths) — Book Review
metadata.color_semantics: claude fidelity: INK #3D3929; SPARK #D97757 one accent.
metadata.author: Seth Brown
metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Seth Brown — Humanitarians AI Fellow. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. The book maps computer algorithms onto real choices like apartment hunting, parking, dating, and home selling. Lab studies by Rappoport and Seale show humans reaching about 31% of optimal performance. People are close to optimal but systematically stop too early rather than too late. The honest limit is that unstructured human problems may not fit these tidy algorithms.

artifactLines: The book maps computer algorithms onto real choices like apartment hunting, parking, dating, and home selling.
Lab studies by Rappoport and Seale show humans reaching about 31% of optimal performance.
People are close to optimal but systematically stop too early rather than too late.
The honest limit is that unstructured human problems may not fit these tidy algorithms.

beats.narration_text: The finding that humans stop at roughly 31% in optimal-stopping problems, close to optimal but systematically early, really stuck with me, alongside the caveat that unstructured human problems may not fit these algorithms. I want to probe that gap. Walk me through: (1) where the mathematically optimal stopping threshold actually sits and why; (2) why real people in Rappoport and Seale's lab quit early rather than late; (3) one messy real-world case, like dating or apartment hunting, where the clean rule genuinely breaks.
